era_context: Philip II continues his military expansion in the Balkans and begins to intervene in Greek affairs. The Persian Achaemenid Empire faces continued instability. The Greek city-states, particularly Athens and Thebes, remain powerful but increasingly threatened by Macedonian expansion. In China, the Warring States period continues.

events.title: Philip II intervenes in Thessalian affairs
events.description: Philip II begins to intervene in the internal conflicts of Thessaly, a region in central Greece. Thessaly is fragmented among competing aristocratic factions, and Philip exploits these divisions to extend Macedonian influence southward. This intervention marks the beginning of Philip's expansion into Greek affairs proper, moving beyond the Balkans. Thessaly's resources and strategic location make it valuable to Philip's ambitions.
events.certainty_note: Philip's intervention in Thessaly is attested in multiple sources, but the precise chronology is debated. Some sources place the beginning of Philip's Thessalian involvement in 357 or 356 BCE.

events.title: Third Sacred War begins in Greece
events.description: The Third Sacred War begins when Phocis seizes control of the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi and its treasury. This war will last approximately ten years and will involve most of the Greek city-states. The conflict provides Philip II with an opportunity to intervene in Greek affairs under the pretext of defending the sanctuary. The war is significant because it demonstrates the fragmentation of the Greek city-state system and creates the conditions for Macedonian dominance.
events.certainty_note: The Third Sacred War is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The date of 357 BCE is widely accepted by modern scholarship.
cross_references: Leads to -346-001 (end of Third Sacred War)

events.title: Social War begins in Athens
events.description: The Social War (also called the Allies' War) begins when several members of the Athenian maritime confederacy rebel against Athenian control. The rebels include Rhodes, Chios, Cos, and Byzantium. The war reflects the decline of Athenian naval power and the unwillingness of allies to remain under Athenian domination. The conflict weakens Athens at a critical moment when Philip II is rising in the north, reducing Athens' ability to respond to the Macedonian threat.
events.certainty_note: The Social War is well-attested in ancient sources. The date of 357 BCE is widely accepted.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of the beginning of Philip's Thessalian intervention is debated, with some sources placing it in 356 BCE. The significance of the Third Sacred War is sometimes overstated; modern scholarship recognizes that it was one of several conflicts that weakened Greek unity. The Social War's impact on Athenian power is sometimes exaggerated; Athens remained a significant power despite the rebellion.
historiographic_note: Sources for 357 BCE are primarily Greek, with significant bias toward Athenian and Theban perspectives. Demosthenes' Philippics are contemporary primary sources but are rhetorical and politically motivated, not objective historical accounts. Later compilations by Diodorus and Pausanias are valuable but written centuries after the events. Persian and Egyptian sources are almost entirely absent from the record for this year. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history.

graph_edges.note: Philip's Thessalian intervention continues to expand
Third Sacred War continues for approximately ten years
Social War continues for approximately two years
Both conflicts weaken Greek unity and create opportunities for Macedonian expansion
